rationale: This is a classic textbook example of the Houben-Hoesch reaction, featuring the acylation of a highly activated, electron-rich aromatic ring (1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene) with a simple nitrile (acetonitrile). The reaction is catalyzed by a Lewis acid (ZnCl2) and a protic acid (HCl), leading to the formation of an aryl ketone (2,4,6-trimethoxyacetophenone). The reactants and product are simple, acyclic where possible, and clearly illustrate the core transformation of this named reaction: electrophilic acylation of an activated arene using a nitrile.
collision_avoidance_note: This example is specific to the Houben-Hoesch reaction, which uses a nitrile as the acylating agent for an electron-rich arene. It is distinct from the Friedel-Crafts acylation (which uses acyl halides or anhydrides), the Gattermann reaction (which uses HCN or its derivatives to form an aldehyde), and the Vilsmeier-Haack reaction (which uses a substituted formamide to form an aldehyde). The use of a simple nitrile and a highly activated polyalkoxybenzene avoids ambiguity with other electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions.
